Wheel.title: Wheel
Wheel.description: Represents a customizable 'wheel of fortune' created by a user, which acts as a container for challenges.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Wheel entity.

name.description: The user-defined name or title for the wheel.

userId.description: Reference to the unique identifier of the user who owns this wheel. (Relationship: User 1:N Wheel)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the wheel was first created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the wheel was last updated.

Challenge.title: Challenge
Challenge.description: Represents an individual challenge, option, or item that can be added to and spun on a specific wheel.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Challenge entity.

text.description: The actual text or description of the challenge item.

wheelId.description: Reference to the Wheel this challenge belongs to. (Relationship: Wheel 1:N Challenge)

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the challenge was first created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the challenge was last updated.

isAI_Generated.description: Indicates if the challenge text was generated by the AI tool.

definition.description: Stores individual Wheel entities. Each wheel is owned by a specific user, identified by the '{userId}' path parameter. The 'userId' in the path ensures authorization independence for security rules.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns this wheel.

params.name: wheelId
params.description: The unique identifier for the Wheel document.

definition.description: Stores individual Challenge entities. Each challenge belongs to a specific wheel, which in turn is owned by a user. The '{userId}' and '{wheelId}' in the path ensure authorization independence and clear ownership from the parent Wheel.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user who owns the parent wheel and thereby this challenge.

params.name: wheelId
params.description: The unique identifier of the parent Wheel document.

params.name: challengeId
params.description: The unique identifier for the Challenge document.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed based on the core principles of Authorization Independence, Structural Segregation, and Path-Based Access Modeling to ensure security, scalability, and debuggability. All user-specific data, specifically 'Wheels' and their associated 'Challenges', are organized under a user's own private subcollection. This ensures that a user's data is inherently private and easily secured. 

1.  **Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):** By structuring data as `/users/{userId}/wheels/{wheelId}` and `/users/{userId}/wheels/{wheelId}/challenges/{challengeId}`, the `userId` is embedded directly into the document's path. This is a crucial aspect of denormalization for authorization. Firestore Security Rules can directly compare `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` wildcard from the path. This eliminates the need for expensive and complex `get()` calls to retrieve parent documents (e.g., a 'Wheel' to determine its owner before authorizing access to a 'Challenge'). This approach ensures atomic operations and simplifies rule debugging significantly.

2.  **QAPs (Query Anything Permissions):** This structure inherently supports secure `list` operations. A user can query all their wheels by accessing `/users/{request.auth.uid}/wheels` without any additional filtering required in security rules, as all documents within that path belong to `request.auth.uid`. Similarly, listing challenges for a specific wheel (`/users/{request.auth.uid}/wheels/{wheelId}/challenges`) is also secure and efficient, as the path itself guarantees ownership context. This prevents unauthorized users from performing wide, unfiltered queries.

3.  **Structural Segregation:** All data within `/users/{userId}` is strictly private to that `userId`. This ensures a homogeneous security posture for all `Wheel` and `Challenge` documents, simplifying security rules to a straightforward `request.auth.uid == userId` check.

4.  **Hierarchical Ownership:** The `1:N` relationship between User and Wheel, and Wheel and Challenge, is naturally represented through nested subcollections. This makes the ownership clear and rules intuitive. Although the `Challenge` schema originally lacked a `userId` field, its placement within `/users/{userId}/wheels/{wheelId}/challenges/{challengeId}` effectively makes `userId` accessible through the path wildcard, thus satisfying the denormalization requirement for authorization.
